content.problemStatement:
The Chef wants to color a **3xN** grid and he has **N** different types of colors to do that. Each cell of the grid has to be colored with exactly one color. A coloring of the grid is
considered beautiful if no two cells of the same row or same column have the same color.



The cells of first two rows are already colored and they don’t violate the beautiful condition (one color doesn’t appear more than once on the same row or on the same column).



Find how many different ways he can color the **3rd** row such that the resulting grid is beautiful. Two ways of coloring are considered different if there is at least one cell which is colored with different color.
constraints: A1, A2, ..., AN is a permutation of the numbers 1, 2, ..., N.
B1, B2, ..., BN is a permutation of the numbers 1, 2, ..., N.
content.inputFormat: First line contains T, the number of test cases. Each test case contains an integer N on the first line, followed by two lines containing N integers each, representing the colors of the first and second rows.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the number of different ways to color the 3rd row such that the resulting grid is beautiful.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first test case, the valid ways to color the 3rd row are: {1,3,2,4} and {3,4,1,2}. For the second test case, the valid ways are: {3,1,4,2}, {3,2,4,1}, {4,1,3,2}, and {4,2,3,1}.
The valid ways to color the 3rd row are: {2,3,1} and {3,1,2}.
The valid ways to color the 3rd row are: {2,4,1,3} and {3,1,4,2}.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved using backtracking. We start by trying to color the first cell of the third row with each of the N colors. For each color, we check if it is valid (i.e., it does not appear in the same column in the first two rows and it does not appear in the same row in the third row). If it is valid, we recursively try to color the rest of the cells in the third row. If we are able to color all cells, we increment the count of valid colorings.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N!) per test case, O(T * N!) total. The time complexity is due to the backtracking algorithm trying all possible permutations of colors for the third row.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — the dominant structure is the recursion stack and the arrays used to store the colors of the rows.

code.Python3:
import sys

N = None
A = None
B = None
C = None
used = None

def isValid(col, color):
    for i in range(col):
        if C[i] == color: return False
    if A[col] == color or B[col] == color: return False
    return True

def backtrack(col):
    if col == N: return True
    for color in range(1, N + 1):
        if used[color]: continue
        if isValid(col, color):
            C[col] = color
            used[color] = True
            if backtrack(col + 1): return True
            used[color] = False
    return False

T = int(input())
while T > 0:
    N = int(input())
    A = list(map(int, input().split()))
    B = list(map(int, input().split()))
    C = [0] * N
    used = [False] * (N + 1)
    count = 0
    for i in range(1, N + 1):
        for j in range(1, N + 1): used[j] = False
        C[0] = i
        used[i] = True
        if backtrack(1): count += 1
    print(count)
    T -= 1
